author: @totherarf
text:
Splicing Jute rope? ...... I'm the very Chap!

Good tip with the insulation tape (black is best as it stretches better). You can cut your zip tie as soon as you have done the first half. When you get near the end cut the individual strands at an angle and force them into the center of the rope by untwisting it a tad.  Once you have all 3 cut to different lengths and put to the center on both ends of the splice, stretch the rope and roll it between your two hands to force the splice to "Lie down". No need to do the string thing .... it will all be good without it! 

A bit of practice and you will have it down pat ...... then we can move on to the Eye Splice ;0) (see what I did there?)

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Thank you for the tips mate! I will definitely keep this in mind for next time, it would look so good with no string! Eye splice is awesome, I might have to do that next. Cheers! :)

author: @Forestduck1
text: I know it's off topic for this video but I have been binge watching this channels videos on batteries and super capacitors and graphene and it has been very interesting.  I just wanted to know if you or anyone else has tried improving on faradays law by dipping a copper coil in graphene and seeing if it improves the amount of energy it produces when a magnet is introduced? or maybe covering a magnet in graphene or both and testing if it improves the results.  Food for thought :)

author: @stevetobias4890
text:
I worked on the boats for 5+ years and learner splicing. We did on under then the next run before doing the second or third under. A bit of heat to melt the frey will save the need for string. We joined ropes, created loops for docking etc.
It's a great skill to learn next to knowing your knots.
Once a splice is done roll it on the floor under your foot to settle it in natural position.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: I really would like to learn more about knots, what an awesome skill to have! The suggestion to roll it on the floor is fantastic! Thanks mate! :)

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: @MyNameIsLukey You're welcome mate, any time.

author: @Earzone63
text:
very handy tip :) 
One question: Is there any harm to leaving in tape / zip ties in terms of the rope's tensile strength, or is it just more 'aesthetically pleasing' to take them off?

replies.author: @Gainn
replies.text:
The glue on most types of tape breaks down eventually, so it could lead to potential loosening issues after long-term use.
Probably wouldn't be very good if it got wet either.
With zip ties it's probably more down to usage.. Anything that involves friction would cause issues with the harder ties potentially damaging the rope, and snagging/damaging anything it runs against. Also a cut risk if it's something you have to pull on.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: what @Gainn said. Awesome explanation! :)

author: @heelerhealer7552
text:
Only other thing to add, would be to give it a roll on the table, like rolling a piece of bread dough. 
Helps the splice settle and wiggles the strands into a bit better alignment. 
Great video, splices are much better than knots, in most cases.
